category.name: parties_and_recitals
category.element: Contracting parties and their legal designations
Party roles (licensor/licensee, producer/performer, publisher/producer, owner/distributor, platform/creator)
Organizational type and jurisdiction of formation

category.name: rights_grants
category.element: Specific rights granted (reproduce, distribute, perform, display, make available, synchronize)
Licensed media and platforms (theatrical, broadcast, SVOD, AVOD, TVOD, FAST, digital, mobile)

category.name: ownership_and_chain_of_title
category.element: Underlying IP ownership (copyright, trademark) and which party holds it
Chain of title — whether rights were properly assigned or licensed at each transfer point
Title gaps, reversions, or known disputes referenced in the agreement

category.name: financial_terms
category.element: Revenue share percentages and calculation methodology
Per diem, travel, and production allowances
Contingent compensation and net profits definitions

category.name: obligations_and_restrictions
category.element: Approval and consultation rights (marketing, key art, credit)
Editorial control rights (cuts, edits, re-versioning, format alteration)

category.name: talent_and_third_party_obligations
category.element: Guild and union compliance (SAG-AFTRA, WGA, DGA, IATSE)
Residual and royalty obligations to performers, writers, directors
Music licensing status (sync rights, master use rights, public performance rights)
Clearances for embedded IP (logos, footage, artwork, brand marks, archival material)
Third-party consent requirements and pending clearances

category.name: digital_and_emerging_rights
category.element: AI training and generative AI usage restrictions or grants
Data privacy and viewer data ownership provisions
NFT, metaverse, and interactive media rights
Social media clip rights and promotional use on digital platforms
User-generated content policies and platform takedown obligations

category.name: legal_protections
category.element: Limitation of liability and damage caps
Insurance requirements (E&O coverage, general liability, production insurance)

category.name: regulatory_and_compliance
category.element: Local content quotas (particularly for international distribution)
Accessibility requirements (closed captioning, audio description, subtitles for the deaf)
Data protection and privacy law compliance (GDPR, CCPA, etc.)

category.name: credit_and_attribution
category.element: Screen credit specifications (position, size, card type)
Music credit requirements in end titles

type.name: content_licensing_agreement
type.indicators: References to "Licensed Content," "Licensed Media," territory grants, SVOD/AVOD/TVOD platform enumeration, holdback provisions, and content delivery obligations.
type.key_provisions: Grant of rights, territory, licensed media, language/dubbing/subtitle rights, holdbacks, license fees, revenue share, minimum guarantees, term and renewal, chain of title warranty, E&O insurance, embedded IP clearances, regulatory compliance.

type.name: talent_performance_agreement
type.indicators: References to "Performer," "Producer," role descriptions, principal photography periods, SAG-AFTRA, residuals, screen credit, morals clauses, pay-or-play terms.
type.key_provisions: Engagement and services, fixed and contingent compensation, rights grant including likeness, AI training restrictions, digital likeness protections, credit, exclusivity, morals clause, key person clause, pay-or-play, guild compliance, social media clip rights.

type.name: music_synchronization_license
type.indicators: References to "Composition," "synchronization," "master recording," "master use license," ASCAP/BMI/SESAC/GMR, sync fees, performance royalties, approved scene descriptions.
type.typical_parties: Music Publisher/Rights Holder and Production Company
type.key_provisions: Composition identification, approved use and scene, duration of use, territory, media, sync fee, performance royalties, MFN, no-alteration clause, promotional use allowance, chain of title warranty for the composition.

type.name: distribution_agreement
type.indicators: References to "Distributor," "Programs," distribution channels, windowing schedules, distribution fees, recoupable expenses, marketing plans, subdistribution, reversion of rights.
type.key_provisions: Appointment and exclusivity, authorized channels, windowing, distribution fee percentage, recoupable expenses, net receipts, marketing approval, delivery requirements, language/dubbing/subtitle rights, editorial control, chain of title warranty, E&O insurance, guild residual obligations, embedded IP clearances, reversion, sell-off period, local content quotas, accessibility requirements, export controls.

type.name: digital_rights_ugc_license
type.indicators: References to "Platform," "Creator," "Creator Content," user-generated content, ad revenue share, subscription pro-rata, direct monetization, community guidelines, content moderation, DMCA, data portability.
type.key_provisions: License to platform (host, store, transcode), promotional license, creator retains ownership, ad revenue share, subscription revenue formula, direct monetization, content standards, moderation and appeal, DMCA compliance, analytics access, data portability, platform amendment rights, AI training restrictions, social media clip rights, viewer data ownership, data privacy compliance.

area.name: financial_structures
area.guidance: Fixed fee / flat fee: A one-time or installment-based payment for rights granted. Common in content licenses and sync licenses.

Revenue share: A percentage of defined revenue (Net Revenue, Gross Receipts, Net Profits). Pay close attention to the definition of the revenue base and approved deductions — these materially affect the economic value.
Minimum guarantee (MG): A guaranteed minimum payment, typically recoupable against future revenue share. The recoupment mechanic determines whether the MG is an advance or a floor.
Distribution fee: A percentage retained by the distributor from gross receipts before any other deductions. Typically ranges from 15-35%.
Recoupable expenses: Costs the distributor may deduct from revenue before remitting to the content owner. Watch for uncapped expense categories.
Most-favored-nations (MFN): Ensures one party receives terms no less favorable than those given to comparable parties. Common in music sync deals.
Pay-or-play: Producer must pay performer's full compensation regardless of whether services are actually used. A significant financial commitment.
Residuals: Ongoing payments for reuse of content, often governed by guild collective bargaining agreements (SAG-AFTRA, DGA, WGA).
Net Profits / Contingent Compensation: Typically defined with extensive deductions. The definition of "Net Profits" is often the most heavily negotiated provision in talent agreements.

Ad revenue share vs. subscription pro-rata: Digital platforms may use different models. Ad share is based on ad impressions against specific content. Subscription pro-rata allocates a pool based on viewing time ratios.

area.name: defined_terms
area.guidance: Defined terms are the backbone of contract interpretation. A term defined in Section 1 controls its meaning throughout the entire agreement.
Watch for terms defined parenthetically within a clause (e.g., 'the "Territory"') as well as terms defined in a dedicated definitions article.

Key defined terms to always capture: Licensed Content, Territory, Licensed Media, Term, Net Revenue/Gross Receipts/Net Profits, Production, Composition, Programs, Creator Content, Community Guidelines.
Blank defined terms (e.g., Territory: _____________) indicate terms requiring negotiation. Flag these prominently.

area.name: windowing
area.guidance: Windowing refers to the sequential release of content across different platforms with exclusivity periods between each window.
Typical window sequence: Theatrical → Premium VOD → SVOD → Free TV/AVOD.
Window durations are measured in days or months post-theatrical release.
Holdbacks prevent the licensor from licensing to competing platforms during specified periods.

area.name: chain_of_title
area.guidance: Chain of title is the documentary evidence that the grantor actually owns or controls the rights being licensed. A clean chain means no gaps, unresolved claims, or competing interests.

Look for representations and warranties specifically addressing ownership and clear title — not all ownership warranties are equal. "Grantor owns all rights" is stronger than "Grantor has the right to enter into this agreement."

Work-for-hire: Under U.S. law, requires either an employment relationship or a signed written agreement for commissioned works in enumerated categories. If the contract references work-for-hire but no written agreement is evidenced, flag this as a risk.
Prior encumbrances: Look for carve-outs or disclosures of existing licenses, liens, or security interests that may limit the rights being granted. These are sometimes buried in exhibits or schedules.
If the contract is silent on chain of title entirely, this is a significant gap — the licensee has no contractual protection if a third party claims ownership.

area.name: language_and_localization
area.guidance: Language rights are critical in international distribution deals. Look for explicit grants of dubbing and subtitling rights, approved languages, and whether the grant is territory-matched.
Cost allocation: Who pays for dubbing and subtitling is a meaningful economic term. Look for whether costs are borne by the distributor, deducted as recoupable expenses, or shared.
Quality approval: Content owners often retain approval rights over localized versions to protect brand integrity. Look for approval mechanics and whether silence constitutes acceptance.

Ownership of localized versions: The party who pays for dubbing/subtitling does not necessarily own the localized version. The contract should specify who owns dubbed tracks and subtitle files upon termination.
If a distribution agreement covers multiple territories but is silent on language rights, flag this as a gap — the distributor may not have authority to create localized versions.

area.name: talent_and_third_party
area.guidance: Guild/union compliance: If the content involves SAG-AFTRA, WGA, DGA, or IATSE talent, the agreement should address who is responsible for residual payments and CBA compliance. Failure to comply can result in guild action against both the producer and the distributor.

Music licensing has three components: (1) synchronization license for the composition, (2) master use license for the specific recording, and (3) public performance rights handled through PROs (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC, GMR). All three must be cleared.

Embedded IP clearances: Every third-party element visible in the content (logos, artwork, signage, archival footage, photographs) requires clearance. The content owner typically warrants that all clearances are obtained.
Pending clearances: Some agreements acknowledge that certain clearances are pending at execution. These should be flagged as risk items requiring resolution before distribution.

area.name: digital_and_emerging
area.guidance: AI and digital likeness: Increasingly common in talent agreements. Look for consent requirements, per-use authorization, and additional compensation triggers for AI-generated replicas of performance, voice, or likeness.

AI training rights: Distinct from digital likeness. Look for whether the licensee may use the content as training data for ML models. Modern agreements increasingly prohibit this explicitly or require separate consent and compensation.

NFT, metaverse, and interactive media: If not explicitly addressed, these rights are generally reserved by the rights holder under most standard grants. Flag their absence as an item to clarify, especially in newer deals.
Social media clip rights: Look for duration limits (typically 30-60 seconds), approved platforms, and whether clips may be independently monetized or are limited to promotional use.

Viewer data ownership: In streaming distribution deals, viewer data has independent commercial value. Look for who owns it, sharing obligations, anonymization requirements, and restrictions on using data for competitive purposes.
Content moderation: In digital/UGC agreements, look for the platform's right to remove, restrict, or demonetize content, and whether the creator has appeal rights.
Data portability: The creator's right to download their content and metadata upon termination. Note format requirements and timelines.
Platform amendment rights: Many digital agreements allow the platform to unilaterally update terms with notice. Note the notice period and whether continued use constitutes acceptance.

Data privacy compliance: Look for references to specific regulatory regimes (GDPR, CCPA, LGPD, PIPA). In international deals, data protection obligations may vary by territory and should be allocated between parties.

area.name: insurance
area.guidance: E&O (Errors and Omissions) insurance is standard in distribution agreements. Typical minimum coverage is $1M-$3M per occurrence / $3M-$5M aggregate. Look for specified minimums and whether they are adequate for the deal size.
The distributor or broadcaster typically requires being named as an additional insured on the E&O policy. Look for this requirement and the deadline for providing proof of coverage.
If the agreement requires insurance but specifies no minimum amounts, flag this as vague — the coverage could be nominal and effectively worthless.
If the agreement is entirely silent on insurance, flag this as a missing standard provision, especially for distribution agreements and talent agreements.

area.name: regulatory_compliance
area.guidance: Content ratings: In broadcast and theatrical distribution, content must be rated by the applicable classification body (MPAA for U.S. theatrical, BBFC for UK, FSK for Germany, etc.). The agreement should specify who is responsible for obtaining ratings and bearing the cost.

Local content quotas: The EU's AVMS Directive requires streaming platforms to ensure at least 30% European works in their catalogs. Australia, Canada, and other jurisdictions have similar requirements. For international distribution deals, look for quota-related obligations.

Accessibility: FCC rules in the U.S. require closed captioning for most video programming. The European Accessibility Act imposes similar requirements in the EU. Look for who is responsible for creating accessible versions and bearing the cost.

Export controls and sanctions: Distribution to sanctioned territories (e.g., OFAC-listed countries) creates legal exposure. Look for compliance representations and territory carve-outs for sanctioned jurisdictions.
If the agreement covers international distribution but is silent on regulatory compliance, flag this as a gap — the parties have not allocated compliance risk.

not_media_rights_contracts.item: General commercial contracts without media/entertainment-specific terms are NOT media rights contracts.
Employment agreements without performance, likeness, or creative rights provisions are NOT talent agreements.
Software licenses or SaaS agreements are NOT media rights contracts.
Real estate leases, loan agreements, or insurance policies are NOT media rights contracts.
Non-disclosure agreements or confidentiality agreements alone are NOT media rights contracts, though they may accompany one.

analytical_principles.principle: Pay special attention to defined terms (typically capitalized or in quotes) and track their definitions throughout the document.
Note references to exhibits, schedules, or attachments and their described contents.
Identify whether provisions are standard boilerplate or appear to be specifically negotiated terms.
When blank fields appear (e.g., $___, ___%), treat them as unfilled terms requiring negotiation.

The same word can have different meanings in different contract types. "Territory" in a distribution agreement refers to geographic scope; in a talent agreement it may refer to where the performer may not compete. Context controls meaning.
The absence of a provision is often as significant as its presence. A distribution agreement with no force majeure clause, or a talent deal with no AI protections, creates risk through silence.
